Thyroid Structure Synthesis Binding & T3/T4 Regulation Metabolism Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase Diseases Blood Pressure Treatment
Dr. Johnson · Clinical Notes

Thyroid Hormones
& Thyroid Disease

A comprehensive review of thyroid physiology, pathology, and clinical management — covering all PLOs and LOs.

PLO 1–4: Basic Science LO 1–5: Clinical Application Graves · Hashimoto's · Iodine Deficiency
01
Basic Structure of the Thyroid Gland
PLO 1

Gross Anatomy

The thyroid gland is a bilobed endocrine gland located in the anterior neck, straddling the trachea. It is the metabolic engine of the body — producing hormones that regulate virtually every tissue.

Microscopic Architecture

The gland is composed of thousands of spherical follicles, each consisting of:

  • A single layer of follicular (epithelial) cells surrounding a central lumen
  • The lumen filled with colloid — mostly thyroglobulin (Tg), the glycoprotein scaffold on which thyroid hormones are assembled
  • Parafollicular (C) cells interspersed between follicles, producing calcitonin

Follicular Cells

Transport iodide, synthesize thyroglobulin, produce T3 and T4, and store hormone in the colloid. Stimulated by TSH.

Thyroid Hormones Produced

T4 (thyroxine) — the dominant secreted form; acts as a prohormone.
T3 (triiodothyronine) — the biologically active form; 3–4× more potent.

Key Concept

Think of T4 as a circulating reservoir of prohormone that peripheral tissues convert to the active T3 on demand. Approximately 80% of circulating T3 comes from extrathyroidal deiodination of T4; only ~20% is directly secreted by the thyroid.

02
Biosynthesis, Storage & Release
PLO 2

The Essential Role of Iodine

Iodine is absolutely required for life — without it, T3 and T4 cannot be synthesized. It is an element that cannot be made by the body and must be obtained exclusively through diet. The primary dietary sources are seafood, dairy, and iodized salt.

Iodide Transport into Follicular Cells

Dietary iodine is absorbed as iodide (I⁻) and transported into follicular cells against an electrochemical gradient by the Na⁺/I⁻ symporter (NIS):

  • Linked to sodium cotransport — energy dependent
  • Requires oxidative metabolism
  • Saturable and regulatable by TSH
Goitrogens

Goitrogens are dietary substances (e.g., cruciferous vegetables like kale, broccoli, cauliflower) that interfere with iodide uptake or incorporation into thyroglobulin. In already-hypothyroid individuals consuming large quantities, this can trigger full-blown hypothyroidism and goiter formation (via ↑TSH stimulation of the gland).

Step-by-Step Biosynthesis

Iodide Trapping: I⁻ enters follicular cell via the Na⁺/I⁻ symporter (NIS) on the basolateral membrane.
Diffusion to Apex: I⁻ diffuses to the apical surface of the follicular cell.
Transport into Colloid: I⁻ is transported into the colloid lumen via pendrin on the apical membrane. (Genetic defects in pendrin → hypothyroidism.)
Oxidation & Organification: Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) oxidizes I⁻ → I⁰ and incorporates iodine into tyrosine residues on thyroglobulin → monoiodotyrosine (MIT) and diiodotyrosine (DIT).
Coupling (also by TPO):
DIT + DIT → T4 (thyroxine — 4 iodines)
MIT + DIT → T3 (triiodothyronine — 3 iodines)
Endocytosis of Thyroglobulin: Tg-containing T3/T4 is taken back into the follicular cell by endocytosis and fused with a lysosome.
Proteolysis & Release: Lysosomal proteases cleave T4 and T3 from Tg. T4, T3 (plus some MIT and DIT) are released into the circulation.
Iodide Recycling: Iodotyrosine deiodinase reclaims iodine from unused MIT and DIT within the cell for reuse.

Thyroid hormone biosynthesis — follicular cell overview

Steps 1–4: Iodide trapping, diffusion, pendrin transport, TPO oxidation/organification

Steps 6–8: Endocytosis, proteolysis, release; ~80% of T3 from peripheral T4 deiodination

Key Enzyme — TPO is a Critical Drug & Disease Target
  • Methimazole & PTU both inhibit TPO → block T3/T4 synthesis (used in Graves' disease)
  • Anti-TPO antibodies are the hallmark of Hashimoto's thyroiditis

Storage

Thyroid hormone is uniquely stored extracellularly in the colloid as thyroglobulin — providing weeks of reserve. When demand increases, TSH stimulates endocytosis of Tg and rapid release of T3/T4 into the bloodstream.

Iodine Deficiency & Global Health

Iodine deficiency is most prevalent in high-altitude mountainous regions (Himalayas, Andes, parts of Africa and Central Europe) whose soils were never covered by ancient seas and thus lack iodine. Consequences include:

  • Goiter — chronic TSH overstimulation enlarges the gland
  • Hypothyroidism — insufficient T3/T4 synthesis
  • Cretinism — the most common preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwide; results from inadequate T3/T4 during fetal brain development

Prevention Salt iodization programs (in the US since the 1920s using KI or CuI). Specialty salts (sea salt, kosher salt) are typically not iodized.

Recommended Dietary Allowances for Iodine
Age GroupMaleFemalePregnantLactating
1–13 years90–120 mcg90–120 mcg——
14+ years150 mcg150 mcg220 mcg ↑290 mcg ↑

Requirements increase in pregnancy and lactation to support fetal/infant brain development.

03
Thyroid Hormone Binding, Transport & T3 vs. T4
PLO 3

Plasma Binding Proteins

Over 99.5% of circulating T4 and T3 are bound to plasma proteins — only the free fraction is biologically active:

  • Thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) — primary carrier
  • Transthyretin (TTR) — formerly called thyroxine-binding prealbumin (TBPA)
  • Albumin
Function of Binding Proteins
  • Maintain free T3 & T4 within narrow limits
  • Act as a large circulating reservoir — hormone rapidly available when binding protein dissociates
  • Only free hormone determines biological activity
Normal Free T4 Reference Range

0.8 – 1.8 ng/dL

Clinical labs measure free T4 and TSH (not total T4) for thyroid assessment.

T3 vs. T4 — Structural & Functional Differences

Property T4 (Thyroxine) T3 (Triiodothyronine)
Iodine atoms 4 3
Biological activity Low (prohormone) High — active form
Receptor binding Weak affinity Much higher affinity for TR-α and TR-β
Primary source Thyroid gland (~100%) ~80% from peripheral deiodination of T4; ~20% direct thyroid secretion
Half-life ~7 days (longer) ~1 day (shorter)

Intracellular Mechanisms

  • T4 and T3 enter cells via diffusion and carrier-mediated transport (MCT8) — the brain absolutely depends on MCT8; genetic MCT8 defects → severe intellectual disability
  • Once inside the cytoplasm, a 3'/5'-monodeiodinase converts T4 → T3 so cytoplasmic levels are roughly equal
  • T3 enters the nucleus and binds thyroid hormone receptors (TR-α and TR-β) — encoded on chromosomes 17 and 3 respectively
  • The TR–T3 complex heterodimerizes with the Retinoid X Receptor (RXR) and binds thyroid response elements (TREs) in DNA → alters gene transcription

T4 and T3 entering target cells — MCT8 transport, intracellular deiodination, nuclear receptor binding

Structure of thyroid hormone receptors TR-α and TR-β; nuclear heterodimer with RXR binds TREs

4 Requirements for T3 Nuclear Action

1 Availability of T3   2 Thyroid hormone receptors (TR-α / TR-β)   3 Cofactor (RXR)   4 DNA regulatory elements (TREs)

Defects in any of these can produce a hypothyroid-like state even with adequate hormone levels.

PTU's Unique Mechanism

Propylthiouracil (PTU) not only inhibits TPO (blocking synthesis) but also inhibits the 5'-monodeiodinase that converts T4 → T3 in peripheral tissues — providing a dual mechanism to rapidly lower active T3 levels. This is especially valuable in thyroid storm.

04
Regulation via Negative Feedback
PLO 4

The HPT Axis

Hypothalamus
→
TRH
→
Anterior Pituitary
→
TSH
→
Thyroid Gland
→
T3 / T4
Free T3 / T4 ↑
⟶
Inhibits both TRH and TSH release
⟶
T3 / T4 ↓

Regulation of thyroid hormone production — HPT axis with negative feedback

Primary Hypothyroidism Pattern

↑ TSH + ↓ free T4

95% of hypothyroidism cases are primary. Thyroid gland fails → loss of negative feedback → pituitary pours out TSH.

Graves' Disease Pattern

↓ TSH + ↑↑ free T4/T3

TRAb mimics TSH → thyroid overproduces → high T4/T3 suppresses pituitary TSH to near zero.

Clinical Pearl — Use TSH as the Therapeutic Target

When treating hypothyroidism with levothyroxine, the goal is to normalize TSH (not T3 or T4 directly). TSH reflects the body's integrated assessment of thyroid hormone sufficiency and is the most sensitive indicator of eu-thyroid status.

Effect of starvation on plasma T3, T4, and reverse-T3 — adaptive metabolic conservation

Starvation Effect on Thyroid Hormones

Negative energy balance → adaptive ↓ T3 and T4 production. This lowers the basal metabolic rate to conserve calories — a survival mechanism. The drop is most pronounced for T3, with a compensatory rise in the inactive reverse T3 (rT3). Changes reverse promptly upon refeeding.

05
Thyroid Hormones & Basal Metabolic Rate
LO 1

Mechanism of BMR Regulation

T3 increases gene expression of Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase in virtually all tissues, driving:

  • ↑ Oxygen consumption
  • ↑ Respiration rate
  • ↑ Body temperature (heat generation)
  • ↑ Overall substrate turnover (carbohydrates, fats, proteins)

Think of thyroid hormone as "the gasoline that runs the metabolic machine."

Parameter Hypothyroidism Hyperthyroidism
BMR ↓ (−40 to −50%) ↑ (up to +30%)
Body weight Unexplained weight gain Unexplained weight loss
Temperature tolerance Cold intolerance Heat intolerance
Fatigue Fatigue, slow movement Restlessness, anxiety

Basal metabolic rate vs. thyroid hormone production rate — hypothyroid (↓) vs. hyperthyroid (↑)

Thermogenesis & Brown Adipose Tissue (BAT)

BAT is the primary site of non-shivering (ATP-independent) thermogenesis. Thyroid hormone:

  • Regulates BAT sensitivity to sympathetic (norepinephrine) stimulation
  • Controls expression of mitochondrial uncoupling protein (UCP) — the key driver of BAT heat production

→ Hypothyroidism: BAT underactive → cold intolerance
→ Hyperthyroidism: BAT overactive → heat intolerance

BAT is located in the neck, supraclavicular, and paravertebral regions in healthy adults.

06
Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase & Neuromuscular Symptoms
LO 2

Myopathy in thyroid disorders — hypothyroidism (left) vs. hyperthyroidism (right) comparison

The Central Role of Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase

T3 stimulates transcription of Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase subunits in all excitable tissues. This pump maintains the resting membrane potential by keeping Na⁺ high extracellularly and K⁺ high intracellularly — the basis of electrical excitability in neurons and muscle.

Tissue Hypothyroidism (↓ Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) Hyperthyroidism (↑ Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase)
Skeletal Muscle Hyporeflexia — delayed relaxation of tendon reflexes
Muscle weakness, cramps, stiffness 		Muscle weakness (thyrotoxic myopathy)
Tremor, increased protein catabolism
GI Smooth Muscle ↓ Smooth muscle activity → constipation ↑ Motility → diarrhea
Skin / Epithelium ↓ Protein synthesis → dry, scaly skin, coarse texture, loss of eyebrows Warm, moist skin
CNS Cognitive dysfunction, depression, slow speech Anxiety, restlessness

Sarcopenia Connection

Thyroid hormone levels decline with age. This contributes to sarcopenia (age-related loss of muscle mass, strength, and quality) by:

  • ↓ Myogenesis
  • Alterations in fiber type — thyroid hormone promotes fast-twitch fiber expression; deficiency favors slower fibers
  • Reduced metabolic activity of muscle tissue

Sarcopenia is multifactorial (GH, sex hormones, TH, physical inactivity) — treating hypothyroidism is one modifiable component.

Major symptoms and signs of hypothyroidism — mechanisms, symptoms, and clinical signs

Classic Hypothyroid Exam Finding

Delayed deep tendon reflex relaxation — specifically the Achilles reflex "hung-up" sign. This directly reflects reduced Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase activity in skeletal muscle due to insufficient T3.

Myxedema & Myxedema Coma

Myxedema: Non-pitting edema caused by accumulation of glycoproteins and carbohydrate-containing substances in the extracellular fluid that trap water. Seen in severe hypothyroidism — puffy facies, periorbital edema, enlarged tongue.

Myxedema Coma: A rare, life-threatening decompensation of hypothyroidism (↓ mental status, hypothermia, bradycardia) precipitated by stressors (infection, MI, surgery, cold exposure). Despite the name, patients often present with confusion/obtundation rather than true coma. Medical emergency requiring IV T4 replacement.

07
Graves', Hashimoto's & Iodine Deficiency
LO 3

🔴 Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Chronic Autoimmune Thyroiditis)

Hypothyroidism Most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions

Affects ~10% of the population; sex ratio 7:1 female predominance; prevalence increases with age.

Hashimoto's thyroiditis: surgical histology showing lymphocytic infiltration, germinal centers, follicular destruction

Pathophysiology

  • Autoimmune destruction of thyroid follicular cells via lymphocytic infiltration (T and B cells)
  • Gradual thyroid failure — progressive ↓ T3/T4
  • May present with or without goiter (high TSH → possible goiter)
  • Histology: lymphocytic infiltration, lymphoid germinal centers, follicular destruction

Diagnostic Markers

  • ↑ Anti-TPO antibodies (90–100%)
  • ↑ Anti-thyroglobulin (Tg) antibodies (80–90%)
  • ↑ TSH
  • ↓ Free T4
  • Anti-TSHR antibodies: rare in Hashimoto's (10–20%)

Thyroid hormone biosynthesis — TPO catalyzes iodide oxidation and coupling; Tg is the precursor scaffold

Why Both TPO and Tg Are Targeted

TPO catalyzes iodide oxidation and coupling → essential for T3/T4 synthesis. Tg is the scaffold on which T3/T4 are built and stored. Antibodies against both disable the hormone production machinery.

Estimated prevalence of antithyroid antibodies across disease states (UpToDate)

🟢 Graves' Disease

Hyperthyroidism Most common cause of hyperthyroidism

A syndrome that may include hyperthyroidism, diffuse goiter, and Graves' orbitopathy (exophthalmos — pathognomonic finding).

Three types of thyrotropin receptor antibodies in Graves' disease: stimulating, blocking, and neutral

Pathophysiology

  • Autoimmune production of TSH Receptor Antibodies (TRAb)
  • TRAb binds and activates the TSH receptor → mimics TSH → uncontrolled T3/T4 synthesis
  • Causes diffuse goiter via continuous stimulation of thyroid growth
  • Three TRAb types: Stimulating (dominant — best fit), Blocking, Neutral

Diagnostic Markers

  • ↑ TRAb (Anti-TSHR antibodies) — 80–95% positive; hallmark
  • Graves' orbitopathy on exam
  • ↓↓ TSH (suppressed by high T3/T4)
  • ↑↑ Free T4 and T3
  • Anti-TPO and anti-Tg may also be elevated (50–80%), but lower than Hashimoto's
  • Diffuse goiter

🟡 Iodine Deficiency

Hypothyroidism Most common preventable cause of intellectual disability

Endemic in mountainous areas (Himalayas, Andes), parts of Africa, Central Europe, and Papua New Guinea. The so-called "Goiter Belt."

Pathophysiology

  • ↓ Dietary iodine → ↓ T3/T4 synthesis
  • ↓ T3/T4 → loss of negative feedback → ↑↑ TSH
  • Chronic TSH overstimulation → thyroid hyperplasia → Goiter
  • In fetal development: ↓ T3/T4 → impaired CNS development → Cretinism

Diagnostic Markers

  • ↑ TSH
  • ↓ Free T4
  • Visible/palpable goiter
  • No anti-TPO or anti-Tg antibodies (not autoimmune)
  • Low urinary iodine (population screening)
  • Endemic geography
Feature Hashimoto's Graves' Disease Iodine Deficiency
Thyroid state Hypothyroid Hyperthyroid Hypothyroid
TSH ↑↑ ↓↓ (suppressed) ↑↑
Free T4 ↓ ↑↑ ↓
Anti-TPO Ab ↑↑↑ (90–100%) ↑ (50–80%) Absent
Anti-Tg Ab ↑↑↑ (80–90%) ↑ (50–70%) Absent
TRAb (Anti-TSHR) Rare (10–20%) ↑↑↑ (80–95%) ← HALLMARK Absent
Goiter Variable Diffuse (from TRAb stimulation) Often prominent
Unique feature Lymphocytic infiltration on biopsy Exophthalmos (Graves' orbitopathy) Endemic geography; cretinism
08
Thyroid Hormone & Blood Pressure
LO 4

Hypothyroidism → Hypertension

Despite bradycardia and ↓ cardiac output, most hypothyroid patients present with diastolic hypertension driven by:

  1. ↓ Endothelial NO / impaired vasodilation + ↓ arterial compliance → ↑ SVR
  2. ↓ β-adrenergic responsiveness → bradycardia + ↓ contractility + ↓ cardiac output

The ↑ SVR dominates the ↓ cardiac output, so the net effect is diastolic hypertension despite a low-output state.

Hyperthyroidism → Hypertension

Hyperthyroid patients also typically present with hypertension, driven by:

  1. ↑↑ Cardiac output (↑ heart rate + ↑ contractility via ↑ β-adrenergic receptors)
  2. ↑ Blood volume

The ↑ cardiac output dominates potential peripheral vasodilation → net systolic hypertension and tachycardia. Risk of atrial fibrillation and stroke.

Interactions between thyroid hormones and the cardiovascular system

Cardiac effects specific to hypothyroidism — ↓ NOS, ↓ β-adrenergic receptors

Cardiac Parameter Hypothyroidism Hyperthyroidism
Heart rate ↓ Bradycardia ↑ Tachycardia / AF
Contractility ↓ (↓ β-adrenergic responsiveness) ↑ (↑ β-AR receptors)
Cardiac output ↓ ↑↑
Systemic vascular resistance ↑ (↓ endothelial NO / impaired vasodilation + ↓ arterial compliance) ↓ (vasodilation)
Blood pressure ↑ Diastolic hypertension (↑ SVR dominates despite ↓ CO) ↑ Systolic hypertension
Dysrhythmia risk Bradyarrhythmias, pericardial effusion Atrial fibrillation, sinus tachycardia
Why Hypothyroidism Can Cause Hypertension

Hypothyroidism → diastolic hypertension. Decreased endothelial NO with impaired vasodilation, plus reduced arterial compliance, raises SVR.

Low-output state Decreased β-adrenergic responsiveness causes bradycardia, reduced contractility, and decreased cardiac output.

Net effect: the increase in SVR dominates, so patients can have diastolic hypertension despite low cardiac output.

Bone Effects

T3 stimulates bone resorption via TR-α receptors on osteoclasts. In hyperthyroidism:

  • ↑ Bone turnover → hypercalciuria → ↑ fracture risk
  • Treatment with antithyroid drugs reduces fracture risk to near-normal

Hypothyroidism has the opposite effect on bone turnover but is associated with Hoffman's syndrome (muscle hypertrophy with weakness).

09
Treatment Strategies
LO 5

Hashimoto's Thyroiditis — Treatment

Lifelong therapy in most cases

Drug: Levothyroxine (Synthetic T4)

  • Mechanism: T4 prohormone → peripheral deiodination → T3 (active)
  • Accounts for ~80% of T3 production
  • Oral administration; excellent absorption
  • Dose titrated to normalize TSH
Goals of Therapy
  • Ameliorate symptoms
  • Normalize serum TSH (eu-thyroid state)
  • Reduce goiter size (if present)
  • Avoid over-treatment (iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis)
⚠ Over-the-Counter Thyroid Supplements

Many OTC "thyroid support" products contain active T4 or thyroid stimulants. Patients taking these alongside prescribed levothyroxine may become hyperthyroid. Always ask about supplement use in hypothyroid patients — unexplained hyperthyroid symptoms in a well-controlled patient should prompt this question.

Graves' Disease — Treatment

First: Beta-blocker for symptom control Then: Definitive therapy

Step 1 — Beta Blocker (Atenolol 25–50 mg/day)

Immediate symptom relief by blocking sympathetic overdrive:

  • Controls palpitations, tachycardia, tremulousness
  • Reduces anxiety and heat intolerance
  • Reduces atrial fibrillation risk
  • Atenolol preferred (long half-life → once daily dosing)

Option A
Thionamides

Drug: Methimazole (preferred) or PTU (1st trimester of pregnancy)

Methimazole mechanism: Inhibits TPO → blocks iodide incorporation into Tg → ↓ T3/T4 synthesis. Once-daily dosing.

PTU mechanism: Inhibits TPO + inhibits 5'-deiodinase → blocks T4→T3 conversion peripherally. Preferred in pregnancy (1st trimester) due to lower teratogenicity.

Pros Chance of remission, no permanent hypothyroidism in some
Cons Side effects (agranulocytosis), requires monitoring, does not guarantee cure

Option B
Radioiodine Ablation

Radioactive ¹³¹I is concentrated in thyroid tissue and destroys it. Results in permanent resolution of hyperthyroidism.

Pros Permanent cure
Cons Permanent hypothyroidism (lifelong levothyroxine), radiation precautions, may worsen orbitopathy

Option C
Surgery (Thyroidectomy)

Surgical removal of the thyroid. Rapid, permanent cure of hyperthyroidism.

Pros Rapid and permanent
Cons Permanent hypothyroidism, risk of iatrogenic hypoparathyroidism (parathyroids in proximity), recurrent laryngeal nerve damage, anesthesia risks, high cost

Treatment of Graves' hyperthyroidism — advantages and disadvantages of thionamides, radioiodine, and surgery (UpToDate)

Clinical Reasoning Question (Dr. Johnson)

"A hyperthyroid patient undergoes thyroidectomy. They are now in a hypothyroid state requiring synthetic thyroid hormone. What parameter would you monitor to determine that you have achieved the correct dose?"

→ Answer: TSH. Normalize TSH into the physiologic range for age/sex — not T3 or T4 directly. A eu-thyroid TSH confirms appropriate negative feedback is restored.

Iodine Deficiency — Treatment & Prevention

Prevention (primary strategy):

  • Universal salt iodization (KI or CuI) — in US since 1920s
  • Iodine supplementation in breads and infant foods
  • Increased RDA during pregnancy and lactation

Treatment of established deficiency:

  • Iodine supplementation (dietary or supplement)
  • If goiter present without severe hypothyroidism: iodine supplementation may resolve it
  • If overt hypothyroidism: levothyroxine (T4) replacement
  • Large goiters may require surgical intervention
  • Cretinism cannot be reversed — prevention in pregnancy is critical
Condition Primary Treatment Mechanism Goal
Hashimoto's Levothyroxine (T4) T4 → T3 by peripheral deiodination Normalize TSH
Graves' (acute) Beta-blocker + Methimazole Block sympathetic effects; inhibit TPO → ↓ T3/T4 Eu-thyroid within 3–8 weeks
Graves' (definitive) Radioiodine ablation or surgery Destroy thyroid tissue → permanent hypothyroidism → manage with T4 Normalize TSH on T4 replacement
Graves' (pregnancy) PTU (1st trimester) Inhibits TPO + T4→T3 conversion; lower teratogenicity than methimazole Mild hyperthyroid → protect fetus
Iodine Deficiency Iodine supplementation ± T4 Restore substrate for T3/T4 synthesis Normalize TSH; prevent goiter/cretinism

Dr. Johnson · UNECOM · Thyroid Hormones & Thyroid Disease

Sources: UpToDate, Ganong's Physiology, Greenspan's Endocrinology
